event: Pre-ceramic Archaic (Ortoiroid) peoples migrate from South America and establish the earliest known settlements on the islands.
Ceramic-making Saladoid Arawak peoples arrive from the Orinoco region, founding agricultural villages and giving Saint Kitts the name Liamuiga ('fertile land').
Later Arawakan and then Kalinago (Carib) peoples occupy the islands, with the Kalinago calling Nevis Oualie ('land of beautiful waters').
Christopher Columbus sights the islands on his second voyage, with the larger island later associated with the name Saint Christopher.
Thomas Warner leads English colonists to settle Saint Kitts, making it the first successful English colony in the Caribbean (the 'mother colony of the West Indies').
French settlers under Pierre Belain d'Esnambuc arrive and the island is partitioned, with the English and French sharing Saint Kitts.
English and French colonists jointly massacre the indigenous Kalinago at Bloody Point to forestall a feared uprising.
Anthony Hilton leads English settlers from Saint Kitts to colonize neighboring Nevis.
Sugar cane cultivation expands rapidly, transforming the islands into plantation economies dependent on enslaved African labor.
A massive earthquake and tsunami devastate Nevis's then-capital Jamestown, contributing to the rise of Charlestown.
A French force under Pierre Le Moyne d'Iberville raids and ransacks Nevis during the War of the Spanish Succession.
The Treaty of Utrecht cedes the entire island of Saint Kitts to Britain, ending the period of Anglo-French partition.
Alexander Hamilton, future founding father of the United States, is born in Charlestown, Nevis.
French forces capture Saint Kitts after the siege of Brimstone Hill before the island is later returned to Britain.
The Treaty of Paris confirms British sovereignty over Saint Kitts and Nevis, ending French claims.
Captain Horatio Nelson marries Frances Nisbet on Nevis while enforcing the Navigation Acts in the Caribbean.
Britain abolishes the transatlantic slave trade, though slavery itself continues on the islands' plantations.
Slavery is abolished throughout the British Empire, emancipating the enslaved population under an apprenticeship system.
Full emancipation takes effect as the apprenticeship system ends, freeing former slaves completely.
Saint Kitts, Nevis, and Anguilla are incorporated into the British Leeward Islands federal colony.
Saint Kitts, Nevis, and Anguilla are formally united into a single presidency within the Leeward Islands.
The St Kitts-Nevis-Anguilla Trades and Labour Union is founded, becoming a driving force for workers' rights and political change.
Sugar workers strike at Buckley's Estate, where police kill several protesters in a pivotal moment for the labour movement.
Robert Bradshaw rises to lead the labour movement, becoming a dominant figure in the islands' politics for decades.
Universal adult suffrage is introduced, dramatically expanding the right to vote.
Saint Kitts-Nevis-Anguilla joins the short-lived West Indies Federation.
The West Indies Federation collapses, returning the islands to direct British colonial administration.
Saint Christopher-Nevis-Anguilla becomes an associated state with full internal self-government, with Robert Bradshaw as premier.
Anguilla rebels against rule from Saint Kitts in the 'Anguilla Revolution,' prompting a British intervention.
Britain resumes direct control of Anguilla, effectively detaching it from Saint Kitts and Nevis.
Anguilla is formally separated from the state, leaving Saint Kitts and Nevis as a two-island union.
Saint Kitts and Nevis gains full independence from the United Kingdom on 19 September, becoming the smallest sovereign nation in the Western Hemisphere.
The newly independent nation joins the United Nations, the Commonwealth of Nations, and the Organisation of Eastern Caribbean States.
Kennedy Simmonds, the country's first prime minister, wins the first post-independence general election.
Denzil Douglas of the Saint Kitts and Nevis Labour Party becomes prime minister, beginning a long tenure.
A Nevis referendum on secession from the union narrowly fails to reach the two-thirds majority required.
Hurricane Georges strikes the islands, causing severe damage to homes, agriculture, and infrastructure.
The government closes the centuries-old state-owned sugar industry, ending Saint Kitts's economic reliance on sugar cane.
Timothy Harris becomes prime minister as the Team Unity coalition ends the long-ruling Labour Party government.
Hurricanes Irma and Maria pass near the islands, causing damage while the nation aids harder-hit Caribbean neighbours.
Terrance Drew of the Saint Kitts and Nevis Labour Party becomes prime minister after winning the general election.
